channel.title: 2. Linux on Lab.Koreanbear|한국곰연구소
channel.description: Recent content in 2. Linux on Lab.Koreanbear|한국곰연구소

item.title: Search Engine 03 | Indexing
item.description:
Introduction

  • NPP :

    • large-scale distributed search engine

      • incremental indexing

      • distributed serving (partition by doc_key)

  • Components

    • GINA-B (GINA Batch)

    • NBS (Npp Build Server, write, w/o replica)

    • NSS (Npp Search Server, read, w/ replica)


1. Document Input Manager (GINA-B, GINA Batch)

  • GINAB :

    • request CRUD of docs to refining system

    • store refined docs into DDBS


2. Document DB Server (DDBS)

  • DDBS :

    • Refined Doc Storage Server

    • A large number of documents can be distributed in shards to multiple servers

item.title: Search Engine 00 | Overview
item.description:
Introduction

  • Search Engine System Overview

    • (1) Collecting System

      • collect articles from various media outlets

    • (2) Refining System

      • preprocess collected docs for indexing system

    • (3) Indexing System

      • Creates an index in a format that search engines can understand

    • (4) Serving System

      • returns a set of documents matching the given query

  • References

    • NPP

    • Search Service Platform Wiki - 서비스 구축 시작하기

    • Kick/admin-tool - wiki


1. Collecting

  • collecting : Collect docs to show from
    • (1) Web Crawler
    • (2) DB (Blog, Cafe)
    • (3) Open API (Youtube, Twitter)

2. Refining (Data Processing)

  • Refining : process various unstructed data into structured data

3. Indexing

  • Indexing : index structured (refined) docs by

item.title: Faiss | Overview
item.description:
Introduction

  • Contents
    • Overview
    • Basic - Brutforce : compare query with all vectors
    • IVF - Inverted File : pruning with IVF and compression with SQ
      • IVF (Inverted File)
      • SQ (Scalar Quantization) : float32 -> uint

1. Overview

  • Faiss is a library for efficient similarity search and clustering of dense vectors.
    • contains algorithms that search in sets of vectors of any size, up to ones that possibly do not fit in RAM.
    • contains supporting code for evaluation and parameter tuning.
    • written in C++ with complete wrappers for Python/numpy.
    • Some of the most useful algorithms are implemented on the GPU.
    • developed primarily at Meta’s Fundamental AI Research group.

2. Basics - Bruteforce

  • Create simple index that compares input query with all vectors
    • w/ memory issues
    • w/o pruning and SQ (no training)
# Prepare embedded vectors
embs = np.random.rand(100000, 512)
# build the faiss-index-inner-product
indexflat = faiss.IndexFlatIP(np.shape(embs)[-1])
index = faiss.IndexIDMap2(indexflat)
# add vectors to the index
faiss.normalize_L2(embs)  # to make cos-sim index
index.add_with_ids(embs, ids)  # pylint: disable=no-value-for-parameter
# Set up GPU and gpu-index
res = faiss.StandardGpuResources()  # use a single GPU
index_gpu = faiss.index_cpu_to_gpu(res, 0, index)

# Prepare input vector
vec = np.random.rand(1, 512) # shape should be in (n,d) format
# search similar indices
distances, indices = index_gpu.search(title_vec, 20) # find 20 nearest neighbors

3. IVF - Inverted File

  • Create IVF (Inverted File) index : search by clustering and inverted file
    • clustering : split (clustering) all vector space into k-cluster
      • improve search performance
    • Inverted File :
      • provides a quantization index (centroid of each cluster) for each vector to be added.
      • compare distances between query and vectors in target cluster
# Prepare embedded vectors
embs = np.random.rand(100000, 512)
# IVF index
num_cluster = 100
quantizer = faiss.IndexFlatL2(np.shape(embs)[-1])  # set metrics to clac distance in cluster
index = faiss.IndexIVFFlat(quantizer, np.shape(embs)[-1], num_cluster)
index.train(embs[:100000])
index.add(embs)

# Prepare input vector
vec = np.random.rand(1, 512) # shape should be in (n,d) format
# search similar indices
distances, indices = app.index_gpu.search(title_vec, 20) # find 20 nearest neighbors

References

  • todo

item.description:

1. Quick Start

item.title: Effective Python $10 | Collaboration
item.description:
Introduction

  • The Python community has established best practices to maximize the maintainability of code over time.

  • Even if you’re working on your own, chances are you’ll be using code written by someone else via the standard library or open source packages.

    • It’s important to understand the mechanisms that make it easy to collaborate with other Python programmers.

Item 82. Know Where to Find Community-Built Modules

  1. The Python Package Index (PyPI) contains a wealth of common packages that are built and maintained by the Python community.

item.title: CheatSheet | k8s
item.description:
Introduction

  • Kubernetes (k8s) : is an open-source system for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications

    • Simply a container orchestration tool like docker swarm

  • Kubernetes Components:

    • Kubernetes Cluster : set of worker machines (nodes)

    • Nodes : host the Pods that are the components of the application workload

    • Pod : the smallest deployable units

  • Contents

    • Pod

    • Service

    • kubectl


1. Pod

  • Pods : are the smallest deployable units of computing that you can create and manage in Kubernetes.

item.title: Effective Python #9 | Testing and Debugging
item.description:
Introduction

  • With Python, you ultimately don’t know if the functions your program calls will be defined at runtime, even when their existence is evident in the source code.

    • This dynamic behavior is both a blessing and a curse.

Item 75. Use repr Strings for Debugging Output

  1. Calling print on built-in Python types produces the human readable string version of a value, which hides type information.

  2. Calling repron built-in Python types produces the printable string version of a value. These repr strings can often be passed to the evalbuilt-in function to get back the original value.

item.title: Effective Python #7 | Concurrency and Parallelism
item.description:
Introduction

  • Concurrency enables a computer to do many different things seemingly at the same time.

  • Parallelism, in contrast, involves actually doing many different things at the same time.

  • Python makes it easy to write concurrent programs in a variety of styles.

    • Threads support a relatively small amount of concurrency,

    • Coroutines enable vast numbers of concurrent functions.

    • Subprocesses can also be used to do parallel work through system calls,


Item 52. Use subprocess to Manage Child Process

  1. Use the subprocess module to run child processes and manage their input and output streams

item.title: Effective Python #8 | Robustness and Performance
item.description:
Introduction

  • Metaclasses are often mentioned in lists of Python’s features, but few understand what the accomplish in practice.
  • The name metaclass vaguely implies a concept above and beyond a class
  • metaclasses let you intercept Python’s class statement and provide special behavior each time a class is defined

Item 44. Use Plain Attributes Instead of Setter and Getter Methods

  1. Define new class interfaces using simple public attributes and avoid defining setter and getter methods.

item.title: Effective Python #8 | Robustness and Performance
item.description:
Introduction

  • Python has built-in features and modules that aid in hardening your programs so they are robust in a wide variety of situations.

  • Python includes many of the algorithms and data structures you need to achieve high performance with minimal effort.


Item 65. Take Advantage of Each BLock in try/except/else/finally

  1. The try/finally compound statement lets you run clean up code regardless of whether exceptions were raised in the tryblock

  2. The else block helps you minimize the amount of code in tryblocks and visually distinguish the success case from the try/except blocks

item.title: NLP #9 | Retrieval Augmented Generation
item.description:
Introduction

  • Basic Concept :
    • LLMs struggle to provide up-to-date information or specific domain knowledge that isn’t included in their training data.
    • RAG addresses these limitations by combining knowledge retrieval with language generation in a unified framework.
    • The core idea behind RAG is to retrieve necessary knowledge from external databases and utilize it to answer questions.
  • Key Components of RAG
    • Query Encoder:
      • A language model that understands the user’s question.
      • It encodes the given question into a vector representation.
    • Knowledge Retriever:
      • Based on the encoded query, it searches for relevant information from an external knowledge base.
      • For example, it may retrieve paragraphs or excerpts from large collections of documents like Wikipedia, news articles, or domain-specific books.
    • Knowledge-Augmented Generator:
      • A language model that uses the retrieved knowledge to generate answers to the question.
      • It functions similarly to a standard LLM but incorporates the retrieved knowledge as additional input to produce more accurate and detailed responses.
  • Overall Steps
    • When a user submits a question, the query encoder converts it into an interpretable format
    • The knowledge retriever uses the encoded question to search for relevant information in an external knowledge base.
    • The retrieved knowledge is passed to the knowledge-augmented generator.
    • The generator produces an answer by leveraging the retrieved information.

1. Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA)

  • docs

2. LDA

  • docs

3. Topic Modeling

  • docs

item.title: AI Powered Search | #2 Working with natural language
item.description:
Introduction

  • In the first chapter, we provided a high-level overview of what it means to build an Al-powered search system.

  • Before we dive into those hands-on examples and specific implementations,

  • in this chapter that we first establish a shared (basis) mental model for the higher level problems we’re trying to solve.

    • Specifically, when it comes to intelligent search, we have to deal with many complexities and nuances in natural language

item.title: Effective Python #5 | Classes and Interfaces
item.description:
Introduction

  • As an OOP language, Python supports a full range of features, such as inheritance, polymorphism, and encapsulation

  • Python’s classes and inheritance make it easy to express a program’s intended behaviors with objects.

    • They allow you to improve and expand functionality over time.

    • They provide flexibility in an environment of changing requirements.


Item 37. Compose Classes Instead of Nesting Many Levels of Build-in Types

  1. Avoid making dictionaries with values that are dictionaries, long tuples, or complex nestings of other build-in types.

item.title: Effective Python #4 | Comprehensions and Generators
item.description:
Introduction

  • Python provides a special syntax, called comprehensions, for succinctly iterating through these types (list, dict, set) and creating derivative data structures

  • This style of processing is extended to functions with generators, which enable a stream of values to be incrementally returned by a function.


Item 27. Use Comprehensions Instead of map and filter

  1. List comprehensions are clearer than the map and filter built-in functions because they don’t require lambda expressions.

item.title: Effective Python #3 | Functions
item.description:
Introduction

  • Functions in Python have a variety of extra features that make a programmer’s life easier.

    • Some are similar to capabilities in other programming languages, but many are unique to Python.

  • These extras can make a function’s purpose more obvious.

    • They can eliminate noise and clarify the intention of callers.

    • They can significantly reduce subtle bugs that are difficult to find.


Item 19. Never Unpack More Than Three Variables When Functions Return Multiple Values

  1. You can have functions return multiple values by putting them in a tuple and having the caller take advantage of Python’s unpacking syntax.

item.title: Effective Python #2 | Lists and Dictionaries
item.description:
Introduction

  • In Python, the most common way to automate repetitive tasks is by using a sequence of values stored in a list type.

  • A natural complement to lists is the dict type, which stores lookup keys mapped to corresponding values (in what is often called an associative array or a hash table


Item 11. Know How to Slice Sequences

  1. Avoid being verbose when slicing: Don’t supply 0 for the start index or the length of the sequence for the end index.

item.title: Effective Python #1 | Pythonic Thinking
item.description:
Introduction

  • The Pythonic style isn’t regimented or enforced by the compiler. It has emerged over time through experience using the language and working with others.

    • Python programmers prefer to be explicit, to choose simple over complex, and to maximize readability.

Item 1. Know Which Version of Python You’re Using

  1. Throughout this book, the majority of example code is in the syntax of Python 3.7 (released in June 2018).

  2. This book also provides some examples in the syntax of Python 3.8 (released in October 2019)
